commentary: Ten sessions this week versus eleven last week, so technically one fewer, but nobody's keeping score when one of those sessions is a 13.5km run and two of them involve open water at Balmoral in a jockstrap. The swim volume held solid with three pool sessions at Prince Alfred, all at 2000m, and the addition of those two little Balmoral dips is exactly the kind of chaotic, sun-drenched, look-at-me energy that makes the whole week more interesting than a spreadsheet. That Saturday run came in at 13.5km against last week's 13.1km, and the heart rate dropped from 159 down to 149, which means he covered more ground with a cooler engine — that's not a fluke, that's a body that's actually adapting. The Vision PT session on Monday and three weight training sessions across the week show the lifting isn't slipping either, and if he's doing all of this while apparently spending his Fridays swimming between buoys in minimal swimwear, then honestly the only thing left to say is that Wednesday was clearly the rest day of a man who has his priorities completely sorted.
metrics_commentary: That HRV jump is genuinely wild — 73 ms against a seven-day average of 52 ms means your nervous system is not just recovered, it is thriving, and your body is basically waving a little flag that says more, please. Six thousand six hundred and seventy metres in the pool is the kind of number that makes a very specific type of man's jaw drop at a pool party, and that VO2 Max sitting at 57.3 is quietly elite, the kind of engine that lets you outlast everyone on the dancefloor and still look good doing it. The run-walk at 13.5 km across one session is the one thing I'm side-eyeing, not because it's bad, but because one session when your body is clearly this ready feels like leaving gifts unopened. Get back out there, babe.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, four times he showed up, no gaps, no excuses — the full target hit and the body is responding accordingly. Four PRs this week: leg press up to 168 kilograms, seated cable row cracking 84, incline dumbbell press climbing to 34.7, and hammer curls jumping from 16.8 to 19.6 — that last one is especially fun because nobody notices hammer curls until suddenly the arms are doing things. Weekly volume came in at 20,124 kilograms against last week's 23,660, which is lower, but four PRs in the same week tells you exactly what that means: the body was working harder on quality, not coasting on quantity. Himbo is not worried about the volume dip — he is, however, extremely pleased about what is happening to those arms.
